summary: Patrick McKenzie describes VaccinateCA, a non-profit he led that expanded into Vaccinate The States, building the country's largest public dataset on COVID-19 vaccine availability. The project started as a hackathon-style effort where volunteers called pharmacies directly to gather ground truth about vaccine stock, then evolved into a data broker between community efforts, government agencies, and large publishers like Google Maps. McKenzie argues that the government's software procurement processes were structurally misaligned with the speed the pandemic demanded, and that a scrappy volunteer effort filled a critical gap. He calls for continued urgency in vaccinating the remaining half of America and helping other nations.
key_points: Ground truth about vaccine availability could only be obtained by calling providers directly, since official databases and county policies were routinely stale or inaccurate
Government software procurement processes move too slowly for pandemic response — counties needed months-long RFPs while VaccinateCA delivered working tools in 30 minutes
The most common publishing platform for vaccine availability in America was Facebook pages, revealing how fragmented and low-tech the public health data infrastructure was
The project evolved from a scrappy volunteer phone bank into a national data broker working with the CDC, Google Maps, and state governments
America developed a COVID-19 vaccine in two days but lacked the relatively pedestrian web application infrastructure to distribute it efficiently
money_quotes: The core insight of VaccinateCA on Day 1 was that, if doses were actually being injected, that the person actually doing the injecting both a) knew whether or not they had more doses and b) to whom they could administer them. Essentially all other data in the ecosystem were stale or lies.

It took America two days—two days—to develop a covid-19 vaccine. That is a triumph worth celebrating. Next time, we should match our world-leading scientific expertise with the relatively pedestrian web application the country needs.
It can't be harder to get vaccinated than it is to buy something on Amazon or sign up for Facebook.
The pandemic moves faster than public digital infrastructure.
It turns out that the most common publishing platform in America for vaccine availability is, I kid you not, a Facebook page.
Many governments need a months-long RFP process to get software written. We had a working URL 30 minutes after they emailed us, and localized it to 7 languages the following day.

This is not the typical startup gig; you'll get no equity, your employer will burn through their funding as quickly as possible, our TAM is declining by millions daily, and successful execution in your job duties will see all of us unemployed within months.
key_insight: When government software procurement moves at bureaucratic speed during a crisis, scrappy volunteer efforts that prioritize ground truth over official data systems can fill critical gaps and save lives.
